responses.strategic_rationale: This residential parasomnia research unit navigates three fundamental tensions: (1) ecological validity vs. safety—naturalistic home-like conditions suppress clinical behavior but create staffing and liability risks; (2) event capture vs. cost efficiency—longer stays guarantee episodes but inflate operational expense; (3) data richness vs. participant burden—intensive multi-night PSG protocols yield gold-standard phenotypes but drive dropouts and reduce recruitment yield. The five levers below directly control these trade-offs by addressing recruitment rigor (inclusion thresholds), facility design (clinical vs. domestic calibration), monitoring intensity (tiered vs. uniform protocols), team structure (labor-intensive vs. lean models), and funding dependency (primary vs. contingent revenue). These levers challenge core assumptions: whether strict pre-screening prevents event capture, whether modest facility expansion can balance safety and authenticity, and whether algorithmic triage can offset understaffing.

levers.name: Participant Pre-Screening Rigor
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict pre-screening (specialist EEG + structured collateral interview + baseline PSG) increases admission lead time by 6–8 weeks. Systemic: Reduces unproductive admissions from ~20% to ~8%, yielding 42 net captured events over 3 years vs. 28 under lenient criteria. Strategic: Tighter phenotyping improves Aim 2 longitudinal coherence and publication impact, but delays enrollment velocity and risks underfilling 8-suite capacity in months 6–12.
options: Baseline PSG required for all referrals; specialist PI interview for every candidate; bed-partner collateral mandatory except single-household exception; protocol ceiling of 15% unproductive admissions triggers criteria review

PSG or actigraphy baseline accepted; structured telephone intake by trained coordinator replaces some in-person interviews; collateral history encouraged but not mandatory; accept 20% unproductive admissions as baseline operational threshold

Open-gate intake with minimal pre-screening beyond self-reported frequency; use first residential night as diagnostic window; escalate full PSG only if preliminary sensor data suggest borderline eligibility; tolerate 30%+ unproductive admissions to maximize enrollment speed
levers.review_lever: Controls Phenotypic Precision vs. Recruitment Velocity. Weakness: The options fail to quantify the cost per captured event under each pathway—strict screening may yield cleaner data but at dramatically higher cost-per-event if the pre-screening itself filters out moderate-frequency episodic participants who would eventually produce events during a longer stay.

levers.name: Residential Environment Calibration
levers.consequences: Immediate: Padding and alarm systems ensure zero serious injuries but risk clinical appearance (reduced naturalistic behavior by ~15–20% per prior homelab studies). Systemic: Minimal modifications (white railings, soft edges, standard door locks) preserve authenticity but increase technician workload for safety vigilance by ~30% and raise liability exposure. Strategic: Authenticity trade-off directly impacts event frequency and morphology—suppressed episodes yield weaker phenotyping; over-clinical design reduces recruitment acceptance and increases dropout risk.
options: Maximize domestic aesthetic: muted paint, family furniture, photographs, bedside lamps, minimal visible safety hardware, soft-edge carpentry, standard bedroom locks, relying on 24/7 technician vigilance and video monitoring for rapid response to out-of-bed events

Balanced integration: padded corridor baseboards in natural color, reinforced window frames disguised as standard frames, silent magnetic door sensors, soft handrails as architectural features, private bathrooms with non-slip surfaces and grab bars styled as towel racks, institutional-grade fire safety compliant with visual discretion

Safety-first clinical design: visible padded walls in clinical color, institutionally-marked emergency equipment, standard hospital-grade bed frames, obvious camera fixtures, intercom systems, bright overhead lighting on motion sensors, explicit safety placards, prioritizing injury prevention and liability defense over behavioral authenticity
levers.review_lever: Controls Authenticity vs. Injury Prevention. Weakness: The options ignore seasonal and individual variation—a participant admitted in winter with depressive features may require different environmental calibration than a summer referral with high-frequency night terrors; the fixed design does not accommodate dynamic safety adjustments mid-stay based on observed behavior.

levers.name: Monitoring Intensity Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Tiered model (headband + mattress nightly; full PSG on nights 1, mid-stay, final, plus escalation) reduces per-participant sensor cost by ~€4,500 and annotation burden by 40%. Systemic: Event-triage algorithm trained on mixed-fidelity data (low-resolution baseline + high-resolution confirmed events) requires validation against dual-scorer agreement on a smaller gold-standard subset; delayed detection of low-amplitude arousals on non-PSG nights risks undercounting. Strategic: Moderate approach balances cost and data quality but depends on algorithm robustness—if triage sensitivity drops below 80%, missed events erode Aim 2 phenotyping.
options: Continuous full-montage PSG every night for all participants: 8 suites × 3 nights avg stay × ~€800/night equipment cost = €19K/participant, maximum fidelity, exhausts technician annotation time, requires 4–5 night technicians per rotation, total staff cost ~€2.3M/3-year budget

Tiered protocol: dry-electrode headband + contact-free sensors nightly; enhanced PSG (full scalp montage + leg EMG) on scheduled nights (entry, mid-stay, exit) and immediately after technician-flagged events; mixed-fidelity annotation with sensitivity/specificity benchmarking; moderates cost to ~€6K/participant and scales to 40–45 participants

Lean continuous monitoring: wearable headband + mattress pressure + video only; full PSG reserved for post-hoc confirmation of algorithm-predicted events; minimal real-time annotation burden, permits 50–60 admissions within 3-year budget, but defers clinical-grade confirmation to back-end analysis and risks event misclassification bias
levers.review_lever: Controls Cost Efficiency vs. Data Fidelity. Weakness: The options assume technician triage capability is static, but technician expertise and fatigue vary across months; a new technician in month 8 may flag events at different sensitivity than an experienced one, creating systematic bias in escalation-night enrollment that is not addressed.

levers.name: Core Team Staffing Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Current 9-person model (3 rotating technicians covering 8 suites at ~€720K/year personnel) achieves safe 1:2.7 technician-to-suite ratio and ~4-hour annotation per night per technician. Systemic: Lean 6-person alternative (2 technicians + algorithm-first triage) reduces personnel cost by €240K/year but increases event-response latency to 8–12 minutes and outsources scoring to external contractor (€120K/year additional), netting only €120K savings. Strategic: Understaffing compromises Aim 1 (safe operations validation) and concentrates hiring risk—loss of one expert technician paralyzes night operations; overstaffing pre-month 12 creates idle capacity and morale friction.
options: Hire 3 technicians immediately on 12-month contracts rolling into year 2 and beyond; supplement with 1.5 FTE postdoc rotation for manual scoring; achieve 1:2.7 ratio, 24/7 safe coverage, real-time annotation, and knowledge retention; total cost €2.3M but front-loads institutional capacity

Hire 2 technicians and 1 research assistant on staggered 6-month initial contracts; operationalize semi-automated event-triage by month 4 to reduce real-time annotation burden; pair with part-time external scoring contractor; enables dynamic scaling from 25 to 45 participants; total cost €1.85M with schedule risk in months 4–6

Minimal core: 1.5 FTE technician (day + on-call weekend coverage) + algorithm-primary monitoring; nighttime coverage by trained study coordinator on rotation; defer scoring to async batch processing by postdocs and external raters; reduces personnel to €1.2M but requires algorithm accuracy ≥90% by month 3 and concentrates sleep-science expertise in postdocs only
levers.review_lever: Controls Operational Safety vs. Cost Containment. Weakness: The options do not address burnout or turnover risk in night-shift roles—no option includes mentorship structure, career development, or peer support mechanisms, so all three pathways risk losing institutional knowledge when technicians leave.

levers.name: Revenue and Funding Diversification
levers.consequences: Immediate: Primary funding (DFG + University of Bonn internal; €3.8M baseline) is acquired by month 3 if proposal is funded; pharmaceutical partnership (optional, non-binding) yields €300–500K if terms align by month 6. Systemic: DFG-only pathway commits to belt-tightening if pharma deal fails—either defer expansion to 12 suites, reduce participant compensation from €80 to €60 per night, or compress year-3 scope; pharma partnership introduces 30-day publication review window and implicit expectation of drug-related phenotyping add-ons. Strategic: Dependency on single funder (DFG) creates vulnerability to mid-project budget cuts or changing priority scores; diversification improves financial resilience but dilutes academic autonomy and adds governance friction.
options: DFG + University of Bonn internal as sole pillars (€3.8M), reject pharmaceutical partnerships, commit to lean operations (8-suite capacity, 50–60 participants max, minimal contingency); full academic control, unencumbered publications, but zero flexibility for cost overruns or expansion

Secure DFG + internal baseline (€3.8M) and actively pursue pharma partnership with clear contractual carve-outs (publication rights, 30-day review window only, no data exclusivity); target €400K non-binding support for RBD sub-protocol; flexible scope if pharma materializes, protected scope if it does not

Pursue DFG + internal + European Clinical Trials Network co-funding (€500K stretch target) + boutique sleep-pharma collaborations (3–4 firms, small contracts €50–100K each, pooled approach); diversified revenue, insulates against single-funder dependency, but requires 12–18 months additional grant-writing and multi-stakeholder governance overhead
levers.review_lever: Controls Autonomy vs. Financial Resilience. Weakness: The options ignore in-kind contributions and cost-sharing—University of Bonn may provide lab space, IT infrastructure, or statistical consulting that could be quantified and leveraged to unlock additional DFG co-funding; none of the pathways explicitly models this institutional upside.

responses.summary: The five levers are well-distributed across recruitment, environment, operations, staffing, and funding—the critical tensions of this research unit. However, three flaws emerge: First, Lever 3 (monitoring intensity) and Lever 4 (staffing) are coupled but not explicitly coordinated; if Lever 3 adopts lean continuous monitoring (requiring high algorithm accuracy), Lever 4 must hire elite postdocs, yet no option pair is labeled as synergistic. Second, no lever addresses participant retention or dropout mitigation beyond compensation—a 6–8 week residential stay carries inherent attrition risk, yet incentive design is not modeled. Third, Lever 1 (pre-screening) assumes fixed event-yield targets (70–80% capture rate) but does not explore whether outpatient actigraphy enrichment—screening candidates with home actigraphy for 2 weeks before admission—could achieve tighter phenotyping with lower pre-screening time burden. Concrete addition: Add to Lever 1 a fourth option: 'Home actigraphy enrichment: all referrals complete 2-week outpatient actigraphy before intake decision; select only candidates with confirmed >1.5 episodes per week; reduces unproductive admissions to ~5% and pre-screening time to 3 weeks, but requires outpatient infrastructure and extends recruitment lead time to 10–12 weeks.'

responses.strategic_rationale: Expanding beyond the initial five levers, this second set addresses secondary but critical trade-offs: data governance and privacy (public sharing vs. protection), phenotyping scope (broad NREM characterization vs. RBD-focused specialization), technological debt (build custom pipeline vs. leverage existing frameworks), participant heterogeneity management (strict homogeneity vs. pragmatic inclusion), and expansion timing (early scaling vs. proven pilot validation). These levers operate at the intersection of scientific ambition and operational feasibility. They challenge whether the unit can simultaneously publish longitudinal NREM phenotypes, develop triage algorithms, maintain clinical-grade RBD protocols, and manage 50–70 participants without fragmenting expertise. The levers expose hidden dependencies: RBD protocol complexity (separate inclusion criteria, neurologic screening, distinct AASM guidance) may cannibalize technician capacity if not carefully ringfenced; data governance decisions made at month 1 determine repository partnerships and publication timelines at month 18; and technology choices (EEG-BIDS compliance, custom vs. off-shelf annotation) lock in staffing profiles and maintenance burden for 3 years.

levers.name: Data Governance and Public Deposition Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: BIDS-compliant EEG dataset with de-identified PSG + sensor streams enables Open Science Framework or PhysioNet deposition by month 24, accelerating secondary analysis and AI-method benchmarking by external groups. Systemic: Public deposition requires governance overhead (data use agreements, re-consenting for secondary use, 6-month pre-publication embargo negotiation) consuming ~200 hours across years 2–3; privacy constraints exclude video, limiting reuse for pose-estimation or behavioral-phenotyping machine-learning. Strategic: Restricted institutional deposition (University of Bonn repository only, 5-year embargo) reduces governance burden by 70% but fragments the field—external sleep neuroscience labs cannot access data, slowing algorithm development and limiting funding leverage for Phase 2 grants.
options: Immediate public deposition: BIDS-compliant EEG, PSG, and sensor streams (video excluded) deposited to PhysioNet or OSF by month 24 with Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license; de-identification protocol reviewed by IRB; data use agreements pre-drafted; enables external benchmarking, increases citation impact, attracts collaborative funding, but requires 250+ hours governance work and assumes robust de-identification success

Tiered deposition with embargo: full dataset (including video) retained in University of Bonn secure repository with 3-year embargo; de-identified physiological data only released to PhysioNet at month 36; video accessible to approved collaborators under formal DCM (data collaboration memorandum); balances privacy and openness, reduces immediate governance burden, but defers external innovation by 3 years

Closed institutional-only model: all raw and processed data remain within University of Bonn and are accessible only to site-affiliated researchers and approved collaborators under strict material transfer agreements; publications include synthetic figures and summary statistics only; maximizes privacy protection and minimizes governance overhead, but forecloses open-science leverage and reduces Phase 2 grant competitiveness in funding environments that prioritize data sharing
levers.review_lever: Controls Open Science Impact vs. Privacy and Governance Burden. Weakness: The options do not account for European data retention law (GDPR Article 17, right to be forgotten)—participants may request deletion of their data years into the project, requiring a deletion protocol and versioning strategy for published datasets that is not addressed in any option.

levers.name: RBD Protocol Integration Scope
levers.consequences: Immediate: Separate RBD arm (dedicated neurologic screening, distinct inclusion criteria per AASM, 8–12 additional participants) adds €400–600K operational cost (neurologist consultation, separate tracking database, distinct annotation rubrics). Systemic: RBD integration requires 15–20% of postdoctoral effort for protocol differentiation and clinical liaison; modest scale (8–12 RBD participants) yields underpowered independent phenotyping analysis unless combined with external RBD cohorts. Strategic: Separate protocol honors RBD's distinct nosology (prodromal Parkinson risk, neurodegenerative trajectory) and enables distinct publications, but fragments institutional focus and delays Aim 2 (between-person patterns) completion if RBD data are analyzed in parallel rather than integrated.
options: Full-scale dedicated RBD arm: recruit 12–15 RBD-diagnosed participants with mandatory neurologic screening, polysomnographic confirmation of REM-atonia loss, separate consent pathway, dedicated annotation rubrics; generate separate RBD phenotyping manuscript; increase operational budget by €550K; publish Aim 2 RBD findings independently by month 30

Integrated RBD cohort within core protocol: include 6–8 RBD-suspected participants recruited via neurology clinic; apply same monitoring and annotation framework as NREM arm but flag REM-sleep episodes separately in analysis; single unified annotation database with RBD subgroup tagging; reduce operational overhead by €200K and simplify staffing, but require postdoctoral expertise in both RBD and NREM phenomenology

RBD as opportunistic secondary analysis: exclude RBD from active recruitment; if clinicians refer RBD-suspected cases, admit to standard NREM protocol with no neurologic pre-screening; analyze RBD cases post-hoc if data quality permits; zero additional budget, but yield is unpredictable and separate RBD publications are unlikely; preserves academic focus on NREM primary aim
levers.review_lever: Controls Scientific Scope vs. Operational Coherence. Weakness: The options assume neurologic screening capacity does not bottleneck—no pathway budgets for neurologist time (€80–120/hour), and if neurology consultants are unavailable, the dedicated and integrated arms collapse to opportunistic-only, undermining recruitment targets.

levers.name: Technology Stack and Annotation Infrastructure
levers.consequences: Immediate: Custom Python pipeline (EEG-BIDS export, real-time event flagging, dual-rater interface) requires 4–6 months development by data engineer at month 0–5. Systemic: Mature off-the-shelf EEGLAB + REDCap stack deployed by month 2 reduces development risk but locks in 3-year licensing (~€30K cumulative) and requires training overhead; custom pipeline yields proprietary tools suitable for Phase 2 commercialization, but diverts engineer from algorithm development. Strategic: Technology choice determines scalability bottleneck—custom pipeline optimized for 8 suites may require re-architecting at 12 suites; off-shelf stack is inflexible but predictable; hybrid approach (BIDS-compliant core + custom event-triage layer) balances innovation and stability but increases integration testing burden.
options: Build custom end-to-end pipeline: Python (MNE, scikit-learn) for EEG processing, custom PostgreSQL database schema, real-time WebSocket event-triage interface, dual-rater annotation GUI; 6-month development, 3-month debugging, zero licensing fees, proprietary tools suitable for spin-off or Phase 2 licensing, but engineering effort front-loads to months 1–9 and algorithm development is deferred

Adopt established EEGLAB + REDCap + XNAT ecosystem: 8-week onboarding, standard annotation workflow, robust ecosystem support, €30K total licensing over 3 years, reduces engineering burden to maintenance only (1 day/week), frees engineer for algorithm work by month 4, but inflexible annotation UI and limited real-time event-triage capability

Hybrid lightweight approach: BIDS-compliant export via MNE (open-source), REDCap for dual-rater scoring interface (already adopted by University of Bonn), minimal custom code for real-time event flagging (300 lines of Python), defers full-pipeline optimization to Phase 2; splits engineering effort evenly between infrastructure and algorithm, moderate licensing cost, faster deployment but requires REDCap customization by site IT and carries integration risk
levers.review_lever: Controls Innovation Velocity vs. Implementation Risk. Weakness: The options do not model training burden—introducing custom or hybrid pipelines requires 20–40 hours of technician onboarding, and if technicians are non-technical (common in clinical settings), adoption time is underestimated and real-time triage is jeopardized.

levers.name: Participant Inclusion Heterogeneity
levers.consequences: Immediate: Narrow inclusion (only primary NREM parasomnias; exclude comorbid sleep apnea, psychiatric disorders, medication use) targets 15–20% of referral base but yields phenotypically homogeneous cohorts (Aim 2 effect estimates with lower variance). Systemic: Pragmatic inclusion (accept mild OSA on stable CPAP, treated depression, stable medications) expands eligible population to 40–50% and simplifies recruitment, but introduces confounding variables requiring stratified analysis and increases annotation complexity (sleep-stage and arousal patterns distorted by comorbidities). Strategic: Homogeneous Aim 2 findings are cleaner publications but narrower clinical relevance; heterogeneous cohorts reflect real-world parasomnia presentations and strengthen Phase 2 intervention generalizability, but complicate longitudinal modeling and require larger sample sizes.
options: Strict phenotypic purity: exclude all comorbidities (sleep apnea AHI >10, any current psychiatric medication, active substance use, prior seizure history); screen aggressively with PSG and collateral history; target 50–55 enrolled participants, all with 'pure' NREM parasomnia; Aim 2 findings are internally valid and suitable for mechanism-focused publications, but recruitment may stall if referral pool is smaller than modeled

Pragmatic inclusion with stratification: accept mild-to-moderate OSA (AHI 10–30 on stable CPAP), treated mood disorders on stable regimen (SSRI, lithium, mood stabilizers), nicotine use, prior resolved seizure disorder; stratify analyses by comorbidity profile post-hoc; enroll 60–70 participants; richer between-person variance suitable for longitudinal prediction modeling, but increases annotation workload (15–20% longer per-night review due to confounded arousals)

Exploratory subgroup design: enroll 40–45 participants with 'pure' NREM parasomnia as primary cohort; separately enroll 10–15 participants with NREM parasomnia + comorbid depression or anxiety (without antidepressant medications) as exploratory subgroup; analyze independently; enables both homogeneous-cohort precision and comorbidity-interaction discovery, but requires separate recruitment pipelines and protocol consent addendum
levers.review_lever: Controls Internal Validity vs. Generalizability. Weakness: The options assume comorbidity assessment is static at enrollment, but participants may initiate new medications (antidepressants for reactive depression, stimulants for emerging ADHD) during a 6–8 week stay, which would retroactively invalidate their inclusion status; no option includes mid-stay re-screening or protocol flexibility.

levers.name: Facility Expansion and Scaling Timeline
levers.consequences: Immediate: Maintain 8-suite capacity through year 2 with option to expand to 12 suites in year 3 if pilot metrics (safety record, event capture rate, technician workload, data quality) validate operations. Systemic: Expansion to 12 suites requires €400K upfront (4 additional suites renovation, equipment, temporary staffing surge), but scales enrollment from ~50 to ~75 participants and spreads fixed facility overhead (rent, utilities, IT) across more billable nights. Strategic: Early expansion (month 12) increases Phase 2 grant leverage (larger dataset, stronger preliminary power estimates) but risks operational overload if year-1 recruitment lags or technician cohort is unstable; deferral to month 30 ensures robust processes but leaves capacity underutilized and forfeits funding-cycle advantage.
options: Aggressive early scaling: open suites 9–12 by month 14 upon demonstration of 30 enrolled participants and ≥20 captured events; hire 1.5 additional technicians by month 12 to staff expansion; total enrollment target 75–85 participants by month 36; increased recruitment velocity and Phase 2 competitive strength, but requires year-1 hiring confidence and higher operational risk if month-12 metrics are marginal

Conservative proof-of-concept model: maintain 8-suite capacity and ~50–60 participant ceiling through month 30; open suites 9–12 only after month 24 external review certifies Aim 1 success (24+ months safe operations, ≥50 adjudicated events, staff retention >80%); defer Phase 2 scoping until expansion is demonstrated; lower risk but leaves capacity idle and compresses year-3 enrollment, reducing Phase 2 dataset size

Modular hybrid expansion: open suites 9–10 at month 16 (modest +25% capacity) contingent on month-12 safety and event-capture metrics; hire 1 FTE additional technician; target 65–70 participants by month 36; defer suites 11–12 to Phase 2 if external review at month 24 endorses further scaling; balances growth ambition with operational caution and preserves decision flexibility
levers.review_lever: Controls Funding Velocity vs. Operational Risk. Weakness: The options do not account for recruitment-rate seasonality—winter months in Germany may see 40% lower referral rates due to clinical preference for day-clinic sleep studies; expansion timing ignores this temporal variation, and early expansion in fall 2026 may face recruitment cliff in winter 2027.

responses.summary: These five additional levers (6–10) expose deeper structural tensions absent from the initial set. Strengths: Lever 7 (RBD scope) and Lever 9 (inclusion heterogeneity) directly address scientific scope creep—common failure mode in early-stage research units. Lever 8 (technology stack) is realistic and operationally concrete; many academic labs fail because they over-engineer infrastructure. Lever 10 (expansion timeline) wisely couples facility growth to validated metrics rather than budget pressure. Weaknesses: First, Lever 6 (data governance) and Lever 7 (RBD integration) interact non-linearly—public deposition of RBD-flagged data raises additional neurologic and neurodegenerative-risk disclosure concerns not addressed. Second, no lever models the interaction between Lever 4 (staffing) and Lever 8 (technology)—if custom pipeline is chosen, the engineer cannot simultaneously mentor technicians on annotation best practices, creating a hidden trade-off. Third, Levers 7–10 all assume stable clinical recruitment, but no lever addresses referral-source diversification or seasonal variation mitigation. Concrete addition: Add to Lever 10 a 'Seasonal buffer option': 'Modulate recruitment targets by quarter (Q1–Q2 target 15 admissions, Q3–Q4 target 8–10 admissions); hire seasonal part-time technician (0.3 FTE) in Q1–Q2 to absorb higher volume; reduces expansion urgency and allows conservative month-18 capacity decision based on full-year recruitment pattern rather than early-year enthusiasm.'

responses.strategic_rationale: This third set of levers addresses often-overlooked operational and scientific sustainability challenges: long-term data stewardship and archival (institutional vs. national vs. international repositories), biomarker discovery prioritization (sleep-stage physiology vs. behavioral-motor classification), quality assurance and inter-rater reliability maintenance (continuous calibration vs. periodic audits), external stakeholder engagement (clinical referrer feedback loops vs. arm's-length research), and contingency planning for high-risk failure modes (recruitment drought, technician attrition, algorithm failure). These levers operate at the level of institutional resilience—they determine whether the unit survives and thrives beyond the 3-year funded period or becomes a stranded asset. They challenge the implicit assumption that once Aim 1 is validated (safe operations) and Aim 2 is complete (longitudinal phenotypes), the research unit becomes self-sustaining; in reality, data archival costs, scoring-expert burnout, and referrer fatigue all accelerate post-year-3 unless proactively managed. The levers expose dependencies on external partnerships (national sleep registries, computational neuroscience consortia) and on individual expertise concentration (the PI and postdocs represent single points of failure for algorithm validation and publication strategy).

levers.name: Long-Term Data Stewardship and Post-Project Archival
levers.consequences: Immediate: Year-1 choice of repository (local NAS + university backup vs. national German Sleep Registry vs. international FAIR-data consortium like LORIS) determines data governance, access protocols, and maintenance cost for 10+ years post-funding. Systemic: Local institutional archival (University of Bonn repository, €15K setup + €5K/year maintenance) concentrates stewardship risk—if university IT deprioritizes sleep data or funding shifts, dataset evaporates; national registry (German Sleep Center Network registry, €30K integration + €8K/year) distributes risk but surrenders some data autonomy. Strategic: Choice made at month 1 locks in infrastructure and governance; deferring archival decisions to month 36 (end-of-project) results in orphaned data, loss of institutional memory, and inability to leverage dataset for Phase 2 renewal—early choice is operationally inconvenient but scientifically essential.
options: University of Bonn institutional repository: EEG-BIDS data + de-identified metadata stored on university NAS with encrypted off-site backup; 10-year retention managed by university digital stewardship office; minimal external governance, full local control, €20K capital + €5K annual; suitable for internal Phase 2 leverage, but data-access sustainability depends on university IT budget priority and lacks external preservation redundancy

National German Sleep Registry integration: participate in multi-center collaborative database (German Sleep Center Network + DFG-funded NFDI Sleep initiative); data deposited with standardized schema; shared stewardship across 8–10 institutions; €35K technical integration + €8K annual participation fee; long-term sustainability assured by DFG governance, enables multi-site Aim 2 comparisons, but requires protocol harmonization and delays local data autonomy by 3–6 months

International FAIR-data infrastructure: deposit BIDS-compliant dataset to LORIS (International Brain Imaging Repository) or similar; geographically redundant cloud storage, versioning, persistent identifiers (DOI), open governance; €25K one-time integration + €2K annual (data hosting); maximum transparency and re-use potential, but requires more rigorous de-identification and loses granular local access control; suitable for highest-impact Phase 2 consortium grants
levers.review_lever: Controls Data Sovereignty vs. Long-Term Sustainability. Weakness: The options do not address data versioning or publication timing—if Aim 2 manuscript is published at month 28 with dataset v1.0, but data-quality issues are discovered at month 34, corrections require re-publishing datasets and alerting external users, creating administrative burden that is not resourced in any pathway.

levers.name: Biomarker and Classification Outcome Prioritization
levers.consequences: Immediate: Designate primary outcome as (A) polysomnographic biomarkers (spectral power, arousal density, sleep-stage cycling) vs. (B) motor/behavioral phenotypes (episode frequency, duration, spatial trajectory, injury risk) vs. (C) integrated phenotype (biomarker + behavior + self-report). Systemic: Biomarker-first approach (A) aligns with sleep-neuroscience publication venues and neuropharmacology trials but requires deeper postdoctoral expertise in spectral analysis and produces findings less applicable to clinical risk stratification. Motor-behavior focus (B) yields clinically actionable risk prediction and appeals to neurology referrers, but requires detailed motion-capture or accelerometry analysis and attracts lower-impact journals. Strategic: Primary outcome choice determines postdoctoral hiring (neurophysiology expert vs. movement-disorder specialist), annotation training (EEG-centric vs. video-centric), and Phase 2 funding positioning (mechanism discovery vs. clinical prediction); pivoting at month 18 is costly.
options: Neurophysiologic biomarker primary outcome: focus postdoctoral effort on EEG spectral characterization, sleep-stage architecture, and arousal microstructure; secondary motor-phenotype analysis; Aim 2 manuscripts target Sleep journal and NeuroImage; suitable for basic-science Phase 2 grants and mechanism-focused pharma partnerships, but requires advanced signal processing and appeals narrowly to sleep neurophysiology community

Motor and behavioral phenotype primary outcome: prioritize video analysis, episode morphology classification, sleep-walking trajectory mapping, and injury/fall risk prediction; secondary EEG archival; Aim 2 manuscripts target Neurology and Sleep Medicine journals; attracts clinical neurology Phase 2 collaborations and funding from safety-oriented foundations, but requires computer-vision postdoc and video-centric annotation

Integrated hierarchical outcome: Aim 2a addresses EEG biomarkers, Aim 2b addresses motor phenotypes, meta-analysis examines biomarker-phenotype correlations; dual-expertise postdoctoral team (neurophysiology + movement neuroscience); publications across both domains; longer Aim 2 timeline (month 28–32 instead of month 24–26), but maximizes versatility for Phase 2 positioning and appeals to diverse funding audiences
levers.review_lever: Controls Publication Impact vs. Clinical Applicability. Weakness: The options assume postdoctoral expertise can be cleanly separated (neurophysiology vs. movement science) or additively combined, but interdisciplinary integration requires shared conceptual framework and frequent co-authorship, increasing mentoring burden on the PI by ~15–20% and delaying first-author postdoc independence.

levers.name: Inter-Rater Reliability Maintenance and Calibration Protocol
levers.consequences: Immediate: Dual-rater annotation (two independent scorers per night) is resource-intensive (€120–150K/year for external scorer time at €50/hour × 2,400–3,000 scoring hours/year). Systemic: Monthly video-conference calibration sessions (PI + both raters review 5–10 discordant cases, update criteria) prevent scoring-drift but add 8 hours/month overhead; annual external audit (independent sleep physician scores 5% random subsample, reports Cohen's kappa) costs €8K but detects systematic bias. Strategic: Skipping calibration or audits saves €20K/year but risks criterion-shift bias—if raters gradually adopt idiosyncratic thresholds, Aim 3 (algorithm validation against human agreement) becomes meaningless because the 'gold standard' drifts over time.
options: Continuous calibration protocol: monthly 2-hour video conference with PI and both raters; review 8–10 discordant cases, update annotation rubric as needed, re-score if consensus shifts; quarterly recalibration on full random sample (30 nights); annual external audit by independent sleep physician; total cost €140K/year for rater time + €8K audit, maintains Cohen's kappa >0.80, but requires PI dedication of ~50 hours/year and presumes rater stability

Periodic audit model: hire two raters on 12-month contracts (non-overlapping, cost €80K/year each); dual-annotation of all nights with no active calibration; conduct inter-rater reliability check at months 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, 36 using random 50-night subsample; if kappa drifts below 0.75, trigger PI-led discussion and re-training; external audit every 18 months (€4K); total cost €175K/year but lower PI involvement (10 hours/year) and relies on periodic metrics

Hybrid lightweight approach: single primary rater (trained postdoc, €70K/year) annotates all nights; random 20% sample re-scored by second rater for reliability audit; monthly PI spot-check of 5 nights; kappa computed quarterly; external audit annual; if kappa <0.75 in any quarter, escalate to dual-annotation until consensus restored; total cost €95K/year, lowest cost but highest risk of undetected criterion drift
levers.review_lever: Controls Scoring Integrity vs. Operational Cost. Weakness: The options assume raters have stable availability and motivation across 36 months, but external scorers frequently take leave (vacation, illness, job change) mid-project; no pathway budgets for rater replacement, and loss of a calibrated rater requires 4–6 weeks re-training, risking kappa stability.

levers.name: Clinical Referrer and Stakeholder Engagement Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Minimal feedback model (publish results in academic journals only) alienates referring clinicians who expect clinical utility feedback; zero-feedback referrers reduce repeat referrals and complicate years 2–3 recruitment. Systemic: Quarterly clinical liaison meetings (PI + study coordinator present aggregate findings to referring neurologists and sleep doctors) builds goodwill, generates referral momentum, and surfaces clinician-reported outcome priorities, but adds 40 hours/year coordination overhead and risks premature disclosure of unpublished findings. Strategic: Engagement model chosen at month 6 determines recruitment sustainability—units with active clinician partnerships achieve 60–70% referral-source retention across 3 years; arms-length models see 30–40% dropout in repeat referrals, forcing recruitment pivoting in year 2.
options: Minimal-engagement model: publish Aim 2 findings in peer-reviewed journals only; send summary manuscripts to referring clinicians at publication; no interim feedback or clinical-liaison meetings; protects intellectual property and avoids premature disclosure, but referring clinicians feel excluded and reduce referrals by ~30% after month 12, forcing pivot to patient self-referral (slower, lower diagnostic accuracy)

Quarterly clinical liaison meetings: PI and study coordinator convene referring neurologists and sleep medicine physicians quarterly to present interim aggregate results (anonymized participant counts, event-capture rates, preliminary phenotype trends) without identifying cases; solicit clinician feedback on participant selection and Aim 2 hypothesis refinement; 40 hours/year coordination, but sustains 75%+ referral-source retention and generates co-authorship opportunities for clinicians on Aim 2 meta-analysis

Active clinical-integration model: establish patient-clinician feedback loop—for each participant, send de-identified episode summary and preliminary risk stratification back to referring clinician within 2 weeks of discharge (contingent on consent and appropriate clinical framing); monthly open-office hours for clinicians to discuss cases; position study as clinical-research partnership; 80 hours/year coordination, maximizes referral retention (80%+), but risks medicolegal liability if risk-assessment feedback is misinterpreted by clinicians as clinical recommendations
levers.review_lever: Controls Recruitment Sustainability vs. Scope Creep. Weakness: The options assume clinical liaison burden is additive, but quarterly meetings create expectation of ongoing communication; if interim findings are presented quarterly, clinicians will expect preliminary Aim 3 (algorithm performance) results before year 2, pressuring the algorithm-development timeline and risking premature external validation on incomplete data.

levers.name: High-Risk Contingency and Failure-Mode Response Planning
levers.consequences: Immediate: Proactive contingency planning (month 1 risk register, monthly PI review, escalation protocols) identifies bottlenecks early; recruitment drought or algorithm failure detected by month 8–10 enables course correction. Systemic: Reactive firefighting (crisis response triggered only when targets miss month-18 checkpoints) compresses decision time and forces expensive emergency measures (emergency funding, expedited hiring, protocol relaxation) in months 18–24. Strategic: Contingency planning is administratively burdensome but statistically reduces probability of grant-failure scenario (enrollment <25 by month 18) from ~12% to ~4%; difference is €400K in sunk cost and 18 months of team morale damage if failure occurs unprepared.
options: Comprehensive pre-planned contingency: month 0–1 risk register identifies 6–8 high-probability failure modes (recruitment <10/month, algorithm sensitivity <70%, technician attrition >30%, data quality <80% scorable nights); monthly PI + coordinator review against leading indicators; pre-drafted escalation protocols (e.g., if recruitment <8/month for 2 consecutive months, activate alternative referral channels and relax inclusion criteria); establish €150K contingency reserve for emergency staffing or protocol modification; €25K administrative setup for risk tracking, but reduces crisis probability by 75%

Periodic risk review (month 6, 12, 18, 24): quarterly checkpoint meetings assess progress against Aim 1–3 targets; if any metric falls >10% below plan, convene rapid problem-solving session; no advance contingency drafting, but decision-making is agile and real-time; lower upfront administrative burden (5 hours total), but requires team availability for crisis response and risks delayed action (4–6 week lag between problem detection and implementation)

Hybrid early-warning model: month 1 risk-register identifies 'red-flag' leading indicators (recruitment below 12/month by month 6, algorithm testing begins month 10 with target sensitivity >60% by month 12); monthly tracking dashboard (simple spreadsheet) shows leading indicators; if any indicator triggers red flag, schedule 48-hour problem-solving call; no detailed contingency plans drafted in advance, but automatic escalation protocols activate early enough to permit mid-course correction
levers.review_lever: Controls Preparedness vs. Flexibility. Weakness: The options assume contingency responses are uniform across all team members, but reaction to enrollment shortfall (relax inclusion criteria) vs. algorithm failure (extend Aim 3 timeline) require different expertise and stakeholder buy-in; no pathway specifies decision authority or approval thresholds for major pivots, risking bottleneck if the PI is unavailable or institutional review is required.

responses.summary: These five levers (11–15) address the 'invisible' infrastructure and governance challenges that separate thriving research units from ones that atrophy post-funding. Strengths: Lever 11 (data stewardship) is unusually prescient—many academic labs produce valuable datasets that are lost or orphaned within 5 years of project completion; locking in archival strategy at month 1 is unusual but highly effective. Lever 15 (contingency planning) reflects operational maturity and directly maps to grant-reviewer confidence. Lever 14 (stakeholder engagement) is pragmatic and recruitment-focused. Weaknesses: First, Lever 12 (biomarker prioritization) and Lever 13 (inter-rater calibration) interact—if biomarker-outcome is chosen, EEG-annotation training dominates and video-annotation decay over time, reducing motor-phenotype secondary-outcome quality; the interaction is not made explicit. Second, Lever 15 (contingency planning) assumes the PI has capacity to lead monthly risk reviews, but if the PI is consumed by IRB submissions (months 1–4) or recruiting participants directly (months 6–12), risk-review discipline lapses. Third, no lever addresses the temporal dynamics of failure—some failures (recruitment drought) emerge gradually; others (technician resignation, algorithm failure) are sudden; contingency-response protocols should differ, but the options do not differentiate by failure-mode onset rate. Concrete addition: Add to Lever 15 a 'Failure-mode-specific response option': 'Differentiate contingency protocols by onset rate—Slow-onset failures (recruitment <10/month detected at month 4–6): activate alternative referral channels, extend stay duration, relax comorbidity exclusions; Fast-onset failures (algorithm sensitivity <70% discovered at month 11 testing): extend Aim 3 timeline to month 36, reduce Aim 3 scope to sensitivity/specificity only (defer full ROC analysis), or pivot to post-hoc human-only classification. Sudden failures (technician departure, facility damage): maintain 1.5-month replacement/repair contingency reserve and pre-identify secondary technician and backup facility.'

levers.name: Participant Pre-Screening Rigor
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict pre-screening (specialist EEG + structured collateral interview + baseline PSG) increases admission lead time by 6–8 weeks. Systemic: Reduces unproductive admissions from ~20% to ~8%, yielding 42 net captured events over 3 years vs. 28 under lenient criteria. Strategic: Tighter phenotyping improves Aim 2 longitudinal coherence and publication impact, but delays enrollment velocity and risks underfilling 8-suite capacity in months 6–12.
options: Baseline PSG required for all referrals; specialist PI interview for every candidate; bed-partner collateral mandatory except single-household exception; protocol ceiling of 15% unproductive admissions triggers criteria review

PSG or actigraphy baseline accepted; structured telephone intake by trained coordinator replaces some in-person interviews; collateral history encouraged but not mandatory; accept 20% unproductive admissions as baseline operational threshold

Open-gate intake with minimal pre-screening beyond self-reported frequency; use first residential night as diagnostic window; escalate full PSG only if preliminary sensor data suggest borderline eligibility; tolerate 30%+ unproductive admissions to maximize enrollment speed
levers.review: Controls Phenotypic Precision vs. Recruitment Velocity. Weakness: The options fail to quantify the cost per captured event under each pathway—strict screening may yield cleaner data but at dramatically higher cost-per-event if the pre-screening itself filters out moderate-frequency episodic participants who would eventually produce events during a longer stay.

levers.name: Residential Environment Calibration
levers.consequences: Immediate: Padding and alarm systems ensure zero serious injuries but risk clinical appearance (reduced naturalistic behavior by ~15–20% per prior homelab studies). Systemic: Minimal modifications (white railings, soft edges, standard door locks) preserve authenticity but increase technician workload for safety vigilance by ~30% and raise liability exposure. Strategic: Authenticity trade-off directly impacts event frequency and morphology—suppressed episodes yield weaker phenotyping; over-clinical design reduces recruitment acceptance and increases dropout risk.
options: Maximize domestic aesthetic: muted paint, family furniture, photographs, bedside lamps, minimal visible safety hardware, soft-edge carpentry, standard bedroom locks, relying on 24/7 technician vigilance and video monitoring for rapid response to out-of-bed events

Balanced integration: padded corridor baseboards in natural color, reinforced window frames disguised as standard frames, silent magnetic door sensors, soft handrails as architectural features, private bathrooms with non-slip surfaces and grab bars styled as towel racks, institutional-grade fire safety compliant with visual discretion

Safety-first clinical design: visible padded walls in clinical color, institutionally-marked emergency equipment, standard hospital-grade bed frames, obvious camera fixtures, intercom systems, bright overhead lighting on motion sensors, explicit safety placards, prioritizing injury prevention and liability defense over behavioral authenticity
levers.review: Controls Authenticity vs. Injury Prevention. Weakness: The options ignore seasonal and individual variation—a participant admitted in winter with depressive features may require different environmental calibration than a summer referral with high-frequency night terrors; the fixed design does not accommodate dynamic safety adjustments mid-stay based on observed behavior.

levers.name: Monitoring Intensity Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Tiered model (headband + mattress nightly; full PSG on nights 1, mid-stay, final, plus escalation) reduces per-participant sensor cost by ~€4,500 and annotation burden by 40%. Systemic: Event-triage algorithm trained on mixed-fidelity data (low-resolution baseline + high-resolution confirmed events) requires validation against dual-scorer agreement on a smaller gold-standard subset; delayed detection of low-amplitude arousals on non-PSG nights risks undercounting. Strategic: Moderate approach balances cost and data quality but depends on algorithm robustness—if triage sensitivity drops below 80%, missed events erode Aim 2 phenotyping.
options: Continuous full-montage PSG every night for all participants: 8 suites × 3 nights avg stay × ~€800/night equipment cost = €19K/participant, maximum fidelity, exhausts technician annotation time, requires 4–5 night technicians per rotation, total staff cost ~€2.3M/3-year budget

Tiered protocol: dry-electrode headband + contact-free sensors nightly; enhanced PSG (full scalp montage + leg EMG) on scheduled nights (entry, mid-stay, exit) and immediately after technician-flagged events; mixed-fidelity annotation with sensitivity/specificity benchmarking; moderates cost to ~€6K/participant and scales to 40–45 participants

Lean continuous monitoring: wearable headband + mattress pressure + video only; full PSG reserved for post-hoc confirmation of algorithm-predicted events; minimal real-time annotation burden, permits 50–60 admissions within 3-year budget, but defers clinical-grade confirmation to back-end analysis and risks event misclassification bias
levers.review: Controls Cost Efficiency vs. Data Fidelity. Weakness: The options assume technician triage capability is static, but technician expertise and fatigue vary across months; a new technician in month 8 may flag events at different sensitivity than an experienced one, creating systematic bias in escalation-night enrollment that is not addressed.

levers.name: Core Team Staffing Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Current 9-person model (3 rotating technicians covering 8 suites at ~€720K/year personnel) achieves safe 1:2.7 technician-to-suite ratio and ~4-hour annotation per night per technician. Systemic: Lean 6-person alternative (2 technicians + algorithm-first triage) reduces personnel cost by €240K/year but increases event-response latency to 8–12 minutes and outsources scoring to external contractor (€120K/year additional), netting only €120K savings. Strategic: Understaffing compromises Aim 1 (safe operations validation) and concentrates hiring risk—loss of one expert technician paralyzes night operations; overstaffing pre-month 12 creates idle capacity and morale friction.
options: Hire 3 technicians immediately on 12-month contracts rolling into year 2 and beyond; supplement with 1.5 FTE postdoc rotation for manual scoring; achieve 1:2.7 ratio, 24/7 safe coverage, real-time annotation, and knowledge retention; total cost €2.3M but front-loads institutional capacity

Hire 2 technicians and 1 research assistant on staggered 6-month initial contracts; operationalize semi-automated event-triage by month 4 to reduce real-time annotation burden; pair with part-time external scoring contractor; enables dynamic scaling from 25 to 45 participants; total cost €1.85M with schedule risk in months 4–6

Minimal core: 1.5 FTE technician (day + on-call weekend coverage) + algorithm-primary monitoring; nighttime coverage by trained study coordinator on rotation; defer scoring to async batch processing by postdocs and external raters; reduces personnel to €1.2M but requires algorithm accuracy ≥90% by month 3 and concentrates sleep-science expertise in postdocs only
levers.review: Controls Operational Safety vs. Cost Containment. Weakness: The options do not address burnout or turnover risk in night-shift roles—no option includes mentorship structure, career development, or peer support mechanisms, so all three pathways risk losing institutional knowledge when technicians leave.

levers.name: Revenue and Funding Diversification
levers.consequences: Immediate: Primary funding (DFG + University of Bonn internal; €3.8M baseline) is acquired by month 3 if proposal is funded; pharmaceutical partnership (optional, non-binding) yields €300–500K if terms align by month 6. Systemic: DFG-only pathway commits to belt-tightening if pharma deal fails—either defer expansion to 12 suites, reduce participant compensation from €80 to €60 per night, or compress year-3 scope; pharma partnership introduces 30-day publication review window and implicit expectation of drug-related phenotyping add-ons. Strategic: Dependency on single funder (DFG) creates vulnerability to mid-project budget cuts or changing priority scores; diversification improves financial resilience but dilutes academic autonomy and adds governance friction.
options: DFG + University of Bonn internal as sole pillars (€3.8M), reject pharmaceutical partnerships, commit to lean operations (8-suite capacity, 50–60 participants max, minimal contingency); full academic control, unencumbered publications, but zero flexibility for cost overruns or expansion

Secure DFG + internal baseline (€3.8M) and actively pursue pharma partnership with clear contractual carve-outs (publication rights, 30-day review window only, no data exclusivity); target €400K non-binding support for RBD sub-protocol; flexible scope if pharma materializes, protected scope if it does not

Pursue DFG + internal + European Clinical Trials Network co-funding (€500K stretch target) + boutique sleep-pharma collaborations (3–4 firms, small contracts €50–100K each, pooled approach); diversified revenue, insulates against single-funder dependency, but requires 12–18 months additional grant-writing and multi-stakeholder governance overhead
levers.review: Controls Autonomy vs. Financial Resilience. Weakness: The options ignore in-kind contributions and cost-sharing—University of Bonn may provide lab space, IT infrastructure, or statistical consulting that could be quantified and leveraged to unlock additional DFG co-funding; none of the pathways explicitly models this institutional upside.

levers.name: Data Governance and Public Deposition Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: BIDS-compliant EEG dataset with de-identified PSG + sensor streams enables Open Science Framework or PhysioNet deposition by month 24, accelerating secondary analysis and AI-method benchmarking by external groups. Systemic: Public deposition requires governance overhead (data use agreements, re-consenting for secondary use, 6-month pre-publication embargo negotiation) consuming ~200 hours across years 2–3; privacy constraints exclude video, limiting reuse for pose-estimation or behavioral-phenotyping machine-learning. Strategic: Restricted institutional deposition (University of Bonn repository only, 5-year embargo) reduces governance burden by 70% but fragments the field—external sleep neuroscience labs cannot access data, slowing algorithm development and limiting funding leverage for Phase 2 grants.
options: Immediate public deposition: BIDS-compliant EEG, PSG, and sensor streams (video excluded) deposited to PhysioNet or OSF by month 24 with Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license; de-identification protocol reviewed by IRB; data use agreements pre-drafted; enables external benchmarking, increases citation impact, attracts collaborative funding, but requires 250+ hours governance work and assumes robust de-identification success

Tiered deposition with embargo: full dataset (including video) retained in University of Bonn secure repository with 3-year embargo; de-identified physiological data only released to PhysioNet at month 36; video accessible to approved collaborators under formal DCM (data collaboration memorandum); balances privacy and openness, reduces immediate governance burden, but defers external innovation by 3 years

Closed institutional-only model: all raw and processed data remain within University of Bonn and are accessible only to site-affiliated researchers and approved collaborators under strict material transfer agreements; publications include synthetic figures and summary statistics only; maximizes privacy protection and minimizes governance overhead, but forecloses open-science leverage and reduces Phase 2 grant competitiveness in funding environments that prioritize data sharing
levers.review: Controls Open Science Impact vs. Privacy and Governance Burden. Weakness: The options do not account for European data retention law (GDPR Article 17, right to be forgotten)—participants may request deletion of their data years into the project, requiring a deletion protocol and versioning strategy for published datasets that is not addressed in any option.

levers.name: RBD Protocol Integration Scope
levers.consequences: Immediate: Separate RBD arm (dedicated neurologic screening, distinct inclusion criteria per AASM, 8–12 additional participants) adds €400–600K operational cost (neurologist consultation, separate tracking database, distinct annotation rubrics). Systemic: RBD integration requires 15–20% of postdoctoral effort for protocol differentiation and clinical liaison; modest scale (8–12 RBD participants) yields underpowered independent phenotyping analysis unless combined with external RBD cohorts. Strategic: Separate protocol honors RBD's distinct nosology (prodromal Parkinson risk, neurodegenerative trajectory) and enables distinct publications, but fragments institutional focus and delays Aim 2 (between-person patterns) completion if RBD data are analyzed in parallel rather than integrated.
options: Full-scale dedicated RBD arm: recruit 12–15 RBD-diagnosed participants with mandatory neurologic screening, polysomnographic confirmation of REM-atonia loss, separate consent pathway, dedicated annotation rubrics; generate separate RBD phenotyping manuscript; increase operational budget by €550K; publish Aim 2 RBD findings independently by month 30

Integrated RBD cohort within core protocol: include 6–8 RBD-suspected participants recruited via neurology clinic; apply same monitoring and annotation framework as NREM arm but flag REM-sleep episodes separately in analysis; single unified annotation database with RBD subgroup tagging; reduce operational overhead by €200K and simplify staffing, but require postdoctoral expertise in both RBD and NREM phenomenology

RBD as opportunistic secondary analysis: exclude RBD from active recruitment; if clinicians refer RBD-suspected cases, admit to standard NREM protocol with no neurologic pre-screening; analyze RBD cases post-hoc if data quality permits; zero additional budget, but yield is unpredictable and separate RBD publications are unlikely; preserves academic focus on NREM primary aim
levers.review: Controls Scientific Scope vs. Operational Coherence. Weakness: The options assume neurologic screening capacity does not bottleneck—no pathway budgets for neurologist time (€80–120/hour), and if neurology consultants are unavailable, the dedicated and integrated arms collapse to opportunistic-only, undermining recruitment targets.

levers.name: Technology Stack and Annotation Infrastructure
levers.consequences: Immediate: Custom Python pipeline (EEG-BIDS export, real-time event flagging, dual-rater interface) requires 4–6 months development by data engineer at month 0–5. Systemic: Mature off-the-shelf EEGLAB + REDCap stack deployed by month 2 reduces development risk but locks in 3-year licensing (~€30K cumulative) and requires training overhead; custom pipeline yields proprietary tools suitable for Phase 2 commercialization, but diverts engineer from algorithm development. Strategic: Technology choice determines scalability bottleneck—custom pipeline optimized for 8 suites may require re-architecting at 12 suites; off-shelf stack is inflexible but predictable; hybrid approach (BIDS-compliant core + custom event-triage layer) balances innovation and stability but increases integration testing burden.
options: Build custom end-to-end pipeline: Python (MNE, scikit-learn) for EEG processing, custom PostgreSQL database schema, real-time WebSocket event-triage interface, dual-rater annotation GUI; 6-month development, 3-month debugging, zero licensing fees, proprietary tools suitable for spin-off or Phase 2 licensing, but engineering effort front-loads to months 1–9 and algorithm development is deferred

Adopt established EEGLAB + REDCap + XNAT ecosystem: 8-week onboarding, standard annotation workflow, robust ecosystem support, €30K total licensing over 3 years, reduces engineering burden to maintenance only (1 day/week), frees engineer for algorithm work by month 4, but inflexible annotation UI and limited real-time event-triage capability

Hybrid lightweight approach: BIDS-compliant export via MNE (open-source), REDCap for dual-rater scoring interface (already adopted by University of Bonn), minimal custom code for real-time event flagging (300 lines of Python), defers full-pipeline optimization to Phase 2; splits engineering effort evenly between infrastructure and algorithm, moderate licensing cost, faster deployment but requires REDCap customization by site IT and carries integration risk
levers.review: Controls Innovation Velocity vs. Implementation Risk. Weakness: The options do not model training burden—introducing custom or hybrid pipelines requires 20–40 hours of technician onboarding, and if technicians are non-technical (common in clinical settings), adoption time is underestimated and real-time triage is jeopardized.

levers.name: Participant Inclusion Heterogeneity
levers.consequences: Immediate: Narrow inclusion (only primary NREM parasomnias; exclude comorbid sleep apnea, psychiatric disorders, medication use) targets 15–20% of referral base but yields phenotypically homogeneous cohorts (Aim 2 effect estimates with lower variance). Systemic: Pragmatic inclusion (accept mild OSA on stable CPAP, treated depression, stable medications) expands eligible population to 40–50% and simplifies recruitment, but introduces confounding variables requiring stratified analysis and increases annotation complexity (sleep-stage and arousal patterns distorted by comorbidities). Strategic: Homogeneous Aim 2 findings are cleaner publications but narrower clinical relevance; heterogeneous cohorts reflect real-world parasomnia presentations and strengthen Phase 2 intervention generalizability, but complicate longitudinal modeling and require larger sample sizes.
options: Strict phenotypic purity: exclude all comorbidities (sleep apnea AHI >10, any current psychiatric medication, active substance use, prior seizure history); screen aggressively with PSG and collateral history; target 50–55 enrolled participants, all with 'pure' NREM parasomnia; Aim 2 findings are internally valid and suitable for mechanism-focused publications, but recruitment may stall if referral pool is smaller than modeled

Pragmatic inclusion with stratification: accept mild-to-moderate OSA (AHI 10–30 on stable CPAP), treated mood disorders on stable regimen (SSRI, lithium, mood stabilizers), nicotine use, prior resolved seizure disorder; stratify analyses by comorbidity profile post-hoc; enroll 60–70 participants; richer between-person variance suitable for longitudinal prediction modeling, but increases annotation workload (15–20% longer per-night review due to confounded arousals)

Exploratory subgroup design: enroll 40–45 participants with 'pure' NREM parasomnia as primary cohort; separately enroll 10–15 participants with NREM parasomnia + comorbid depression or anxiety (without antidepressant medications) as exploratory subgroup; analyze independently; enables both homogeneous-cohort precision and comorbidity-interaction discovery, but requires separate recruitment pipelines and protocol consent addendum
levers.review: Controls Internal Validity vs. Generalizability. Weakness: The options assume comorbidity assessment is static at enrollment, but participants may initiate new medications (antidepressants for reactive depression, stimulants for emerging ADHD) during a 6–8 week stay, which would retroactively invalidate their inclusion status; no option includes mid-stay re-screening or protocol flexibility.

levers.name: Facility Expansion and Scaling Timeline
levers.consequences: Immediate: Maintain 8-suite capacity through year 2 with option to expand to 12 suites in year 3 if pilot metrics (safety record, event capture rate, technician workload, data quality) validate operations. Systemic: Expansion to 12 suites requires €400K upfront (4 additional suites renovation, equipment, temporary staffing surge), but scales enrollment from ~50 to ~75 participants and spreads fixed facility overhead (rent, utilities, IT) across more billable nights. Strategic: Early expansion (month 12) increases Phase 2 grant leverage (larger dataset, stronger preliminary power estimates) but risks operational overload if year-1 recruitment lags or technician cohort is unstable; deferral to month 30 ensures robust processes but leaves capacity underutilized and forfeits funding-cycle advantage.
options: Aggressive early scaling: open suites 9–12 by month 14 upon demonstration of 30 enrolled participants and ≥20 captured events; hire 1.5 additional technicians by month 12 to staff expansion; total enrollment target 75–85 participants by month 36; increased recruitment velocity and Phase 2 competitive strength, but requires year-1 hiring confidence and higher operational risk if month-12 metrics are marginal

Conservative proof-of-concept model: maintain 8-suite capacity and ~50–60 participant ceiling through month 30; open suites 9–12 only after month 24 external review certifies Aim 1 success (24+ months safe operations, ≥50 adjudicated events, staff retention >80%); defer Phase 2 scoping until expansion is demonstrated; lower risk but leaves capacity idle and compresses year-3 enrollment, reducing Phase 2 dataset size

Modular hybrid expansion: open suites 9–10 at month 16 (modest +25% capacity) contingent on month-12 safety and event-capture metrics; hire 1 FTE additional technician; target 65–70 participants by month 36; defer suites 11–12 to Phase 2 if external review at month 24 endorses further scaling; balances growth ambition with operational caution and preserves decision flexibility
levers.review: Controls Funding Velocity vs. Operational Risk. Weakness: The options do not account for recruitment-rate seasonality—winter months in Germany may see 40% lower referral rates due to clinical preference for day-clinic sleep studies; expansion timing ignores this temporal variation, and early expansion in fall 2026 may face recruitment cliff in winter 2027.

levers.name: Long-Term Data Stewardship and Post-Project Archival
levers.consequences: Immediate: Year-1 choice of repository (local NAS + university backup vs. national German Sleep Registry vs. international FAIR-data consortium like LORIS) determines data governance, access protocols, and maintenance cost for 10+ years post-funding. Systemic: Local institutional archival (University of Bonn repository, €15K setup + €5K/year maintenance) concentrates stewardship risk—if university IT deprioritizes sleep data or funding shifts, dataset evaporates; national registry (German Sleep Center Network registry, €30K integration + €8K/year) distributes risk but surrenders some data autonomy. Strategic: Choice made at month 1 locks in infrastructure and governance; deferring archival decisions to month 36 (end-of-project) results in orphaned data, loss of institutional memory, and inability to leverage dataset for Phase 2 renewal—early choice is operationally inconvenient but scientifically essential.
options: University of Bonn institutional repository: EEG-BIDS data + de-identified metadata stored on university NAS with encrypted off-site backup; 10-year retention managed by university digital stewardship office; minimal external governance, full local control, €20K capital + €5K annual; suitable for internal Phase 2 leverage, but data-access sustainability depends on university IT budget priority and lacks external preservation redundancy

National German Sleep Registry integration: participate in multi-center collaborative database (German Sleep Center Network + DFG-funded NFDI Sleep initiative); data deposited with standardized schema; shared stewardship across 8–10 institutions; €35K technical integration + €8K annual participation fee; long-term sustainability assured by DFG governance, enables multi-site Aim 2 comparisons, but requires protocol harmonization and delays local data autonomy by 3–6 months

International FAIR-data infrastructure: deposit BIDS-compliant dataset to LORIS (International Brain Imaging Repository) or similar; geographically redundant cloud storage, versioning, persistent identifiers (DOI), open governance; €25K one-time integration + €2K annual (data hosting); maximum transparency and re-use potential, but requires more rigorous de-identification and loses granular local access control; suitable for highest-impact Phase 2 consortium grants
levers.review: Controls Data Sovereignty vs. Long-Term Sustainability. Weakness: The options do not address data versioning or publication timing—if Aim 2 manuscript is published at month 28 with dataset v1.0, but data-quality issues are discovered at month 34, corrections require re-publishing datasets and alerting external users, creating administrative burden that is not resourced in any pathway.

levers.name: Biomarker and Classification Outcome Prioritization
levers.consequences: Immediate: Designate primary outcome as (A) polysomnographic biomarkers (spectral power, arousal density, sleep-stage cycling) vs. (B) motor/behavioral phenotypes (episode frequency, duration, spatial trajectory, injury risk) vs. (C) integrated phenotype (biomarker + behavior + self-report). Systemic: Biomarker-first approach (A) aligns with sleep-neuroscience publication venues and neuropharmacology trials but requires deeper postdoctoral expertise in spectral analysis and produces findings less applicable to clinical risk stratification. Motor-behavior focus (B) yields clinically actionable risk prediction and appeals to neurology referrers, but requires detailed motion-capture or accelerometry analysis and attracts lower-impact journals. Strategic: Primary outcome choice determines postdoctoral hiring (neurophysiology expert vs. movement-disorder specialist), annotation training (EEG-centric vs. video-centric), and Phase 2 funding positioning (mechanism discovery vs. clinical prediction); pivoting at month 18 is costly.
options: Neurophysiologic biomarker primary outcome: focus postdoctoral effort on EEG spectral characterization, sleep-stage architecture, and arousal microstructure; secondary motor-phenotype analysis; Aim 2 manuscripts target Sleep journal and NeuroImage; suitable for basic-science Phase 2 grants and mechanism-focused pharma partnerships, but requires advanced signal processing and appeals narrowly to sleep neurophysiology community

Motor and behavioral phenotype primary outcome: prioritize video analysis, episode morphology classification, sleep-walking trajectory mapping, and injury/fall risk prediction; secondary EEG archival; Aim 2 manuscripts target Neurology and Sleep Medicine journals; attracts clinical neurology Phase 2 collaborations and funding from safety-oriented foundations, but requires computer-vision postdoc and video-centric annotation

Integrated hierarchical outcome: Aim 2a addresses EEG biomarkers, Aim 2b addresses motor phenotypes, meta-analysis examines biomarker-phenotype correlations; dual-expertise postdoctoral team (neurophysiology + movement neuroscience); publications across both domains; longer Aim 2 timeline (month 28–32 instead of month 24–26), but maximizes versatility for Phase 2 positioning and appeals to diverse funding audiences
levers.review: Controls Publication Impact vs. Clinical Applicability. Weakness: The options assume postdoctoral expertise can be cleanly separated (neurophysiology vs. movement science) or additively combined, but interdisciplinary integration requires shared conceptual framework and frequent co-authorship, increasing mentoring burden on the PI by ~15–20% and delaying first-author postdoc independence.

levers.name: Inter-Rater Reliability Maintenance and Calibration Protocol
levers.consequences: Immediate: Dual-rater annotation (two independent scorers per night) is resource-intensive (€120–150K/year for external scorer time at €50/hour × 2,400–3,000 scoring hours/year). Systemic: Monthly video-conference calibration sessions (PI + both raters review 5–10 discordant cases, update criteria) prevent scoring-drift but add 8 hours/month overhead; annual external audit (independent sleep physician scores 5% random subsample, reports Cohen's kappa) costs €8K but detects systematic bias. Strategic: Skipping calibration or audits saves €20K/year but risks criterion-shift bias—if raters gradually adopt idiosyncratic thresholds, Aim 3 (algorithm validation against human agreement) becomes meaningless because the 'gold standard' drifts over time.
options: Continuous calibration protocol: monthly 2-hour video conference with PI and both raters; review 8–10 discordant cases, update annotation rubric as needed, re-score if consensus shifts; quarterly recalibration on full random sample (30 nights); annual external audit by independent sleep physician; total cost €140K/year for rater time + €8K audit, maintains Cohen's kappa >0.80, but requires PI dedication of ~50 hours/year and presumes rater stability

Periodic audit model: hire two raters on 12-month contracts (non-overlapping, cost €80K/year each); dual-annotation of all nights with no active calibration; conduct inter-rater reliability check at months 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, 36 using random 50-night subsample; if kappa drifts below 0.75, trigger PI-led discussion and re-training; external audit every 18 months (€4K); total cost €175K/year but lower PI involvement (10 hours/year) and relies on periodic metrics

Hybrid lightweight approach: single primary rater (trained postdoc, €70K/year) annotates all nights; random 20% sample re-scored by second rater for reliability audit; monthly PI spot-check of 5 nights; kappa computed quarterly; external audit annual; if kappa <0.75 in any quarter, escalate to dual-annotation until consensus restored; total cost €95K/year, lowest cost but highest risk of undetected criterion drift
levers.review: Controls Scoring Integrity vs. Operational Cost. Weakness: The options assume raters have stable availability and motivation across 36 months, but external scorers frequently take leave (vacation, illness, job change) mid-project; no pathway budgets for rater replacement, and loss of a calibrated rater requires 4–6 weeks re-training, risking kappa stability.

levers.name: Clinical Referrer and Stakeholder Engagement Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Minimal feedback model (publish results in academic journals only) alienates referring clinicians who expect clinical utility feedback; zero-feedback referrers reduce repeat referrals and complicate years 2–3 recruitment. Systemic: Quarterly clinical liaison meetings (PI + study coordinator present aggregate findings to referring neurologists and sleep doctors) builds goodwill, generates referral momentum, and surfaces clinician-reported outcome priorities, but adds 40 hours/year coordination overhead and risks premature disclosure of unpublished findings. Strategic: Engagement model chosen at month 6 determines recruitment sustainability—units with active clinician partnerships achieve 60–70% referral-source retention across 3 years; arms-length models see 30–40% dropout in repeat referrals, forcing recruitment pivoting in year 2.
options: Minimal-engagement model: publish Aim 2 findings in peer-reviewed journals only; send summary manuscripts to referring clinicians at publication; no interim feedback or clinical-liaison meetings; protects intellectual property and avoids premature disclosure, but referring clinicians feel excluded and reduce referrals by ~30% after month 12, forcing pivot to patient self-referral (slower, lower diagnostic accuracy)

Quarterly clinical liaison meetings: PI and study coordinator convene referring neurologists and sleep medicine physicians quarterly to present interim aggregate results (anonymized participant counts, event-capture rates, preliminary phenotype trends) without identifying cases; solicit clinician feedback on participant selection and Aim 2 hypothesis refinement; 40 hours/year coordination, but sustains 75%+ referral-source retention and generates co-authorship opportunities for clinicians on Aim 2 meta-analysis

Active clinical-integration model: establish patient-clinician feedback loop—for each participant, send de-identified episode summary and preliminary risk stratification back to referring clinician within 2 weeks of discharge (contingent on consent and appropriate clinical framing); monthly open-office hours for clinicians to discuss cases; position study as clinical-research partnership; 80 hours/year coordination, maximizes referral retention (80%+), but risks medicolegal liability if risk-assessment feedback is misinterpreted by clinicians as clinical recommendations
levers.review: Controls Recruitment Sustainability vs. Scope Creep. Weakness: The options assume clinical liaison burden is additive, but quarterly meetings create expectation of ongoing communication; if interim findings are presented quarterly, clinicians will expect preliminary Aim 3 (algorithm performance) results before year 2, pressuring the algorithm-development timeline and risking premature external validation on incomplete data.

levers.name: High-Risk Contingency and Failure-Mode Response Planning
levers.consequences: Immediate: Proactive contingency planning (month 1 risk register, monthly PI review, escalation protocols) identifies bottlenecks early; recruitment drought or algorithm failure detected by month 8–10 enables course correction. Systemic: Reactive firefighting (crisis response triggered only when targets miss month-18 checkpoints) compresses decision time and forces expensive emergency measures (emergency funding, expedited hiring, protocol relaxation) in months 18–24. Strategic: Contingency planning is administratively burdensome but statistically reduces probability of grant-failure scenario (enrollment <25 by month 18) from ~12% to ~4%; difference is €400K in sunk cost and 18 months of team morale damage if failure occurs unprepared.
